caveats: a: The OEWS source covers all foundries, not 331523.
a: The estimate excludes gains requiring new physical automation rather than AI substitution or avoided hiring.
rho: Published technical feasibility does not establish economical deployment in LMM plants.
rho: Environmental and customer controls limit autonomous changes to furnace and casting parameters.
e: No observed eligibility denominator was found.
e: The injected n=81 is an ESTIMATE using a machinery margin rather than a die-casting-specific margin.
s5: Owner-age data are all-industry and from data year 2018.
s5: Owner age is a succession-pressure proxy rather than an observed transfer probability.
q: No contract-level retention dataset was found.
q: Retention varies by customer concentration, qualification difficulty, tooling ownership, and available foundry capacity.
d5: Foundry-wide value output is not constant-quality die-casting volume.
d5: Automotive program mix can make individual firms diverge sharply from the industry trend.
o: Process substitution affects quantity and belongs primarily in d5.
o: Digital customer interfaces can remove transaction work without eliminating the foundry operator.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring business-to-business programs priced per part or lot, usually supported by tooling, qualification, quality requirements, metal surcharges, purchase orders, releases, and annual or multiyear supply agreements.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive foundries, firms that cast and further manufacture their own finished products, primary or secondary metal producers without contract die casting, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Independent US nonferrous die-casting foundries in the approximately $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band repeatedly producing customer-designed cast components under external supply programs.

a.bridge: BLS foundry occupations and national casting-machine projections describe broader populations, not wage-weighted current task exposure in 331523. Production, maintenance, inspection, and material handling were treated as largely physical, while estimating, engineering documentation, scheduling, procurement, quality analysis, maintenance knowledge, and administration were treated as partly exposed; this may overstate opportunity where process controls and ERP already automate these steps.
caveats: The OEWS source covers all foundries, not 331523.
The estimate excludes gains requiring new physical automation rather than AI substitution or avoided hiring.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task census from representative independent die casters, documenting current machine automation and manual hours in quoting, engineering, scheduling, quality, maintenance, administration, production, and material handling.
a.rationale: Die casting centers on molten-metal handling, machine setup and tending, die maintenance, trimming, finishing, inspection, and internal logistics. AI can assist information and diagnostic tasks, but only a minority of wages support work that can be substituted without new robotics or capital equipment.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for all foundries, not 331523 die-casting quantity, to decline 0.8% annually through 2034. Applying that trajectory to a five-year die-casting basket can overstate decline if lightweight nonferrous substitution grows and understate it if internal-combustion powertrain exposure or offshore sourcing is high.
caveats: Foundry-wide value output is not constant-quality die-casting volume.
Automotive program mix can make individual firms diverge sharply from the industry trend.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year North American die-casting shipment forecasts by alloy and end market, reconciled with customer program awards, vehicle powertrain exposure, reshoring, import penetration, and announced capacity.
d5.rationale: BLS projects foundry real output from $26.9 billion in 2024 to $24.8 billion in 2034 and employment down 14.7%. Die casting may benefit from lightweight, consolidated components, but industrial cycles, imports, OEM concentration, and changing vehicle architectures justify a below-one central ratio and wide range.

caveats: No observed eligibility denominator was found.
The injected n=81 is an ESTIMATE using a machinery margin rather than a die-casting-specific margin.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the frozen n population showing captive status, external revenue, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, tooling ownership, equipment condition, environmental liabilities, certifications, and stand-alone management.
e.rationale: Independent die casters ordinarily supply recurring external component programs and fit the operating lens. Captive facilities, product manufacturers with internal casting, heavy customer concentration, environmental liabilities, obsolete equipment, weak stand-alone management, and size-band misclassification remove a meaningful share.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 331523 ($1,387,497,179) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 331523 ($7,415,362,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $260,362 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 331523); class <5: 48 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,188,396 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $233,163 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,685,455 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $330,686 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,846,073 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $558,400 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 81 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,248,370 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,814,530 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 41 firms, avg revenue/firm $54,435,829 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $10,680,310 -> out of $1-10M band; class 500+: 29 firms, avg revenue/firm $144,361,483 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $28,323,723 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 81

caveats: Process substitution affects quantity and belongs primarily in d5.
Digital customer interfaces can remove transaction work without eliminating the foundry operator.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers broadly self-manufacturing die castings, additive or alternative processes eliminating die-cast components, or autonomous foundries operating without accountable production and quality management.
o.rationale: Qualified die-cast parts are physical products requiring accountable molten-metal control, die setup and maintenance, process execution, traceability, inspection, and shipment. Software can assist and equipment can automate tasks, but customers still require an accountable manufacturer within five years.

caveats: No contract-level retention dataset was found.
Retention varies by customer concentration, qualification difficulty, tooling ownership, and available foundry capacity.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of customer contracts and program margin histories showing metal pass-through, productivity-sharing or cost-down clauses, renewal cadence, rebids, and margin persistence after documented operational improvements.
q.rationale: Foundries can retain benefits from lower scrap, faster launches, better uptime, and fewer quality escapes, especially during fixed-price program periods. OEM cost-down demands, competitive sourcing, annual negotiations, open-book costing, metal surcharges, and program rebids pass a substantial share back to customers over five years.

caveats: Published technical feasibility does not establish economical deployment in LMM plants.
Environmental and customer controls limit autonomous changes to furnace and casting parameters.
rho.change_evidence: Audited die-casting deployments reporting labor hours, quote time, scrap, first-pass yield, downtime, false inspection results, customer approvals, and required human-review rates before and after AI implementation.
rho.rationale: Quoting support, document generation, schedule optimization, defect-pattern analysis, maintenance retrieval, purchasing, and routine administration can be implemented within five years. Legacy systems, scarce labeled data, safety, customer validation, cybersecurity, traceability, and human quality release constrain realized use.

s5.bridge: Census reports owner ages across responding US employer businesses, not five-year control transfers for die casters. A fraction of the 51% age-55-plus owner population plus ordinary strategic sales is translated into qualifying transfers after excluding internal succession, closure, and nontransferable assets; this may overstate sales for family shops and understate them where customer qualification favors consolidation.
caveats: Owner-age data are all-industry and from data year 2018.
Owner age is a succession-pressure proxy rather than an observed transfer probability.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year database of completed, failed, and withdrawn transactions for US die casters in the EBITDA band, paired with owner-age, succession-plan, environmental, and customer-consent interviews.
s5.rationale: Broad owner aging supports succession pressure, while qualified customer programs, tooling, and installed equipment can support transfer. Environmental diligence, reinvestment needs, customer concentration, and change-of-control approval can prevent or delay sales.

principal_driver: Repeat engineered programs create recurring quoting, quality, maintenance, scheduling, and documentation workflows around a durable physical service.
principal_weakness: Physical production dominates labor while projected foundry contraction and OEM bargaining power constrain durable retained gains.

sources.supports: Census defines the industry as introducing purchased molten nonferrous metal under high pressure into molds or dies to make castings and excludes establishments that further manufacture castings into finished products.
sources.title: NAICS 331523: Nonferrous Metal Die-Casting Foundries

sources.supports: BLS provides employer-survey occupation and wage estimates for foundries across production, maintenance, engineering, management, and administrative occupations, used as the broader occupation-mix proxy.
sources.title: Foundries - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates

sources.supports: BLS reports 154,600 molding, coremaking, and casting machine setters, operators, and tenders in 2024 and projects 148,800 in 2034, a 3.8% decline.
sources.title: Occupational projections and worker characteristics, 2024-2034

sources.supports: EPA identifies furnace and related secondary-aluminum operations as sources of organic and inorganic hazardous air pollutants and particulate metals subject to process and emissions controls, supporting compliance and accountability constraints.
sources.title: Secondary Aluminum Production: National Emission Standards for Hazardous Air Pollutants

sources.supports: The 2019 Annual Business Survey infographic reports 51% of responding employer-business owners were age 55 or older; estimates cover 4.1 million responding owners in data year 2018.
sources.title: Business Owners' Ages

sources.supports: BLS projects foundry employment declining from 106,000 in 2024 to 90,400 in 2034 and real output from $26.9 billion to $24.8 billion, a 0.8% annual output decline.
sources.title: Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034

title: Nonferrous Metal Die-Casting Foundries
